title: The Bottleneck Problem in Translation
concept: A translation encoder compresses a 50-word sentence into a 300-float vector; the decoder fails because it has to guess the early details.

exercises.prompt: What does the term 'bottleneck' mean in general terms?

options.text: A constraint that limits information flow
A type of fast computer memory

exercises.explain: A bottleneck limits the flow of information, similar to how the narrow neck of a bottle slows down the liquid pouring out.

cards.title: Lost in Compression
cards.body: Have you ever tried to summarize a whole book in just one sentence? You end up leaving out a lot of the best details. Translation AIs face the exact same struggle!

cards.title: What is the Bottleneck?
cards.body: A translation bottleneck happens when an encoder tries to squeeze a very long sentence (like 50 words) into a single, small list of numbers (a vector). Because the space is so tight, crucial details get lost.

cards.title: The Tiny Backpack
cards.body: Imagine squeezing an entire family's vacation clothes into one tiny backpack. By the time you unpack at your hotel, the clothes are hopelessly wrinkled and you realize you forgot the keys. The tiny backpack is the fixed-size vector!

cards.title: A Squished Sentence
cards.body: If you translate: "The fluffy cat, who loved sleeping on the warm radiator all afternoon, chased a mouse," a bottlenecked decoder might output: "A cat chased a mouse," completely forgetting the radiator and the warm afternoon.

cards.title: Myth: AI Never Forgets
cards.body: People think AI brains have infinite memory. In reality, early translation systems had a strict limit. If the input sentence was too long, the AI would simply "forget" the beginning by the time it reached the end.

cards.title: The Bottleneck Visualized
cards.body: See how trying to fit a long sentence into one small vector space limits the decoder's view.

left.text: 50 detailed words containing multiple adjectives and verbs.

right.text: Only 300 numbers trying to represent every single detail at once.

exercises.prompt: What happens when you force a 50-word sentence into a small 300-float vector?

options.text: The decoder translates it perfectly without any errors.
The encoder refuses to process the sentence.
The decoder struggles and has to guess early details.
The model translates only the last word.

exercises.explain: When a long sentence is compressed too much, the decoder has to guess the early details because they were squeezed out during encoding.

exercises.prompt: True or False: The bottleneck problem occurs because the encoder compresses the entire input sentence into a single fixed-size representation.
exercises.explain: Traditional encoder-decoder models squeeze everything into one fixed-size vector, creating a tight bottleneck for long sentences.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to describe how information is squished.
exercises.sentence: The encoder squeezes the whole sentence into a single fixed-size [blank].
exercises.explain: To pass information to the decoder, the encoder compresses the entire sentence into a single, fixed-size vector.

exercises.prompt: Which translation output shows the bottleneck problem in action?

options.text: Input: "A dog barked." -> AI: "A dog barked."
Input: "The fluffy cat on the warm radiator chased a mouse." -> AI: "A cat chased a mouse."

exercises.explain: The second option shows a loss of detail (forgetting the descriptive parts about the fluffy cat and the warm radiator), which is a classic symptom of the bottleneck problem.

exercises.prompt: Why does a bottle neck occur in traditional encoder-decoder translation models?

options.text: The decoder gets too much information at once.
Information gets lost during compression into a single fixed-size vector.
The encoder refuses to process sentences longer than five words.
The translation vector changes size randomly for every word.

exercises.explain: The bottleneck forces the encoder to compress a massive amount of information into a single fixed-size vector. This causes the decoder to lose track of the beginning details by the time it finishes reading the sentence.
